concepts.definition: The strategic process of evaluating market demand, customer needs, competitive landscape, and organizational capabilities to decide which products a company should offer for sale.
concepts.explanation: Determining what products to sell requires analyzing multiple factors including customer demand, market trends, competitive positioning, and the company's internal capabilities. Companies use tools such as market research, customer surveys, demand forecasting, and portfolio analysis frameworks like the BCG Growth-Share Matrix to evaluate product viability. This decision directly impacts supply chain planning, as it drives sourcing, production, inventory, and distribution requirements. Cross-functional coordination among purchasing, operations, marketing, and sales departments is essential to align product selection with overall business strategy. The goal is to identify products that maximize revenue and profitability while meeting customer needs.

sources.title: Product Portfolio Management 101: Your Ultimate Portfolio Planning Guide
5 Steps for Effective Product Portfolio Management

relationships.description: Product selection depends on understanding what products are used and sold by the business
The product portfolio is the output of determining what products to sell

concepts.topic: How a company determines what products are used and sold by a business
concepts.definition: The analytical process by which a company evaluates its current product offerings, customer buying behavior, and market conditions to understand which products are actively consumed, resold, or distributed within its operations.
concepts.explanation: Companies determine what products are used and sold through a combination of internal data analysis and external market research. Internally, businesses review sales data, inventory turnover rates, and customer purchase histories to identify which products generate the most demand and revenue. Externally, they conduct market research including customer surveys, competitive analysis, and industry trend monitoring to understand evolving needs. Product lifecycle analysis helps categorize items by their stage of maturity, from introduction through growth, maturity, and decline. This ongoing assessment informs decisions about product development, discontinuation, and resource allocation across the supply chain.

sources.title: Product Portfolio Management: What It Is and Why It Matters for Business Leaders

relationships.description: Understanding current product usage informs decisions about what products to sell
Analyzing product usage data helps identify slow-moving products headed toward obsolescence

concepts.definition: A product portfolio is the complete collection of all products and services offered by a company, analyzed collectively to assess performance, profitability, and strategic alignment.
concepts.explanation: A product portfolio provides a holistic view of a company's entire range of offerings, serving as a critical management tool for evaluating how each product contributes to overall business objectives. By examining products collectively rather than individually, companies can identify their best- and worst-performing items, allocate resources more effectively, and make informed decisions about product investment, development, or discontinuation. Portfolio analysis helps organizations diversify their offerings to minimize the risk of relying too heavily on a single product or market segment. Common frameworks for portfolio analysis include the BCG Growth-Share Matrix, which categorizes products as stars, cash cows, question marks, or dogs based on market growth and market share.

sources.title: Product Portfolio: What is a Product Portfolio?

relationships.description: The product portfolio reflects the outcome of product selection decisions
The Pareto principle helps analyze which items in the portfolio drive the most value

concepts.definition: The Pareto principle, also known as the 80/20 rule, states that roughly 80% of outcomes or effects result from approximately 20% of causes or inputs.
concepts.explanation: Formulated by Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto in the late 19th century, the Pareto principle was originally observed in the distribution of wealth, where about 20% of the population held approximately 80% of the economic resources. In supply chain and inventory management, this principle is applied through ABC analysis, where approximately 20% of inventory items (Category A) typically account for about 80% of total sales revenue or consumption value. By identifying these high-impact items, supply chain managers can prioritize resources, optimize stock levels, and focus attention where it matters most. This approach reduces the risk of stockouts on critical items while avoiding overstocking of lower-value products, leading to better inventory turnover and reduced carrying costs. The principle underscores that not all products in a portfolio contribute equally, making differentiated management strategies essential.

sources.title: How to Use the Pareto Principle (The 80/20 Rule)
Pareto law: optimizing logistics processes with the 80/20 rule
Understanding the 80/20 Rule: The Pareto Principle in Business

relationships.description: The Pareto principle is used to analyze product portfolio performance and prioritize items
The 80/20 analysis can help identify slow-moving products that fall outside the top-performing category

concepts.definition: Slow-moving products are inventory items with low turnover rates that are declining in demand and progressing toward obsolescence, where they will have little or no market value and are unlikely to be sold in the future.
concepts.explanation: Slow-moving inventory consists of products that sell at a rate significantly below expected levels, tying up capital and warehouse space that could be used more productively. These items typically follow a progression from slow-moving to excess to fully obsolete inventory. Identifying slow-moving products requires monitoring key metrics such as inventory turnover ratios, days of supply, sales velocity trends, and product lifecycle stage. Early identification is critical because it allows companies to take corrective action such as price markdowns, promotional campaigns, product bundling, or planned phase-outs before the inventory loses all value. Failure to manage slow-moving inventory results in increased holding costs, potential write-offs, and reduced supply chain efficiency.

sources.title: Slow-Moving Inventory: Identify, Manage & Prevent It
Calculate SLOB Inventory: Slow Moving And Obsolete Inventory
Slow Moving and Obsolete Inventory Investment

relationships.description: Pareto analysis helps identify slow-moving items that fall in the lower-value inventory categories
Managing slow-moving inventory supports the broader goal of minimizing inventory across the supply chain

concepts.topic: Importance of minimizing inventory during all stages of supply chain management
concepts.definition: Minimizing inventory across all stages of the supply chain is a strategic objective focused on reducing excess stock at every point from raw materials through finished goods, in order to lower costs, reduce waste, and improve operational efficiency.
concepts.explanation: Inventory represents one of the largest capital investments in a supply chain, and excess inventory at any stage increases holding costs including warehousing, insurance, depreciation, and the risk of obsolescence. Effective supply chain management aims to maintain just enough inventory to meet demand without overstocking, a principle central to lean and just-in-time methodologies. Minimizing inventory across all stages, including raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods, improves cash flow, reduces waste, and increases responsiveness to changing market conditions. This requires accurate demand forecasting, strong supplier relationships, and well-coordinated logistics to ensure that reduced inventory levels do not lead to stockouts or service failures. According to CSCMP, effective supply chain management integrates supply and demand management within and across companies to minimize costs, waste, and time throughout the production cycle.

sources.title: CSCMP Supply Chain Management Definitions and Glossary

relationships.description: Identifying and eliminating slow-moving inventory is a key tactic for minimizing overall inventory
The Pareto principle helps prioritize which inventory items to focus on when optimizing stock levels
